content.text: Inner-source quality bars and review standards define the minimum criteria that a contribution must meet before it is merged into a shared inner-source repository. The quality bar has two layers: automated gates enforced by the CI pipeline (test coverage thresholds, type checking, security scanning, API compatibility checks) and human review criteria evaluated by trusted committers (design appropriateness, test quality, API clarity, documentation completeness, backward compatibility). Together, these layers ensure that shared libraries remain high-quality and trustworthy as more contributors from more teams add to them.

Quality bars for inner-source libraries are typically higher than those for single-team codebases. A bug in a team's own service affects that team; a bug in a shared library affects every team consuming it. Inadequate documentation in a team's service is inconvenient for the team; inadequate documentation in a shared library forces every consuming team to read source code rather than docs. The higher bar reflects the wider impact of shared code quality on the organization.

Without explicit quality bars, contribution quality in a popular inner-source repository drifts toward the lowest common denominator over time. Early contributions may be high-quality because they come from experienced engineers who care about the library. Later contributions — as the library becomes widely used and contributors come from many different teams with different standards — may be lower-quality unless the bar is documented and enforced. The result is a library that becomes harder to understand and maintain as it grows.

Explicit quality bars also protect contributors from subjective rejection. When a trusted committer rejects a contribution saying only that it does not meet standards, without being able to point to a specific, documented standard, contributors experience the rejection as arbitrary and become less likely to contribute again. Documented quality bars make rejection decisions transparent and educable: the committer can say exactly which criterion was not met and what the contributor needs to change to meet it.
Consistent code quality across contributions from many different teams with different internal standards.
Automated gates eliminate subjective debates about test coverage or type safety in PR review.
Documented standards enable contributors to self-assess before submitting, reducing review cycles.
High quality bar for shared code reduces the rate of regressions and bugs that affect multiple consuming teams.
New trusted committers can learn the review standard from the documentation rather than through trial and error.
Quality metrics (coverage, SAST findings, API compatibility) provide objective evidence of library health over time.
High quality bars increase the effort required for each contribution and may discourage smaller contributions like documentation improvements.
Automated gates can fail on acceptable contributions if thresholds are calibrated incorrectly (e.g., coverage gate failing because a new function is complex to test).
Different programming languages have different tooling maturity, making it harder to apply identical bars across Python and JavaScript libraries.
Quality bars must be maintained as the library evolves; a bar that was appropriate when the library had 5 public functions may be inadequate when it has 50.
Trusted committers must be calibrated on the quality bar to apply it consistently; inconsistent human review is worse than a lower but consistently applied bar.

Automated quality gates run on every PR before human review begins. The CI pipeline enforces: test coverage must be above a defined threshold (typically 90% for shared libraries, measured per-file as well as overall), all tests must pass on all supported platform versions (Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, for example), type checking must produce no errors on the new or modified files, static security analysis must produce no new findings above the defined severity threshold, and a breaking change detector (based on API diff) must flag any public API changes for explicit acknowledgment.

Human review criteria are documented in the CONTRIBUTING.md and in a separate REVIEW-STANDARDS.md document that trusted committers use as a reference. The criteria cover four dimensions: correctness (do the tests actually cover the behavior, not just achieve high coverage by testing happy paths?), API design (is the new API consistent with existing naming conventions, is the surface area minimal, could this be an optional parameter rather than a new function?), documentation (does every public function have a docstring with at least one example, is the CHANGELOG entry accurate?), and security (does the new code introduce any new network calls, file I/O, or subprocess invocations that should be documented and potentially restricted?).

Trusted committers use a review checklist when evaluating PRs, not an open-ended assessment. The checklist creates consistency: two trusted committers reviewing the same PR should reach the same conclusion on whether each criterion is met, even if they would make different design choices themselves. When a committer declines a contribution, the review comment must reference the specific checklist item that was not met and provide concrete guidance on what change would address the concern.

attrs.alt: Inner-source quality bars and review standards flow

content.text: Define quality bars before the first external contribution, not after problems have already accumulated. A library that accepted ten low-coverage contributions before defining a coverage requirement will need to retroactively increase coverage for those contributions when the bar is enforced going forward, which is harder than enforcing it from the start. The investment in defining quality bars at repository creation pays off immediately on the first contribution.

Review quality bars annually and after any significant incident caused by a contribution that passed the existing bar. If a contribution that met all documented criteria caused a regression in production, the bar was inadequate and should be raised. If no contributions are failing the bar (all PRs merge cleanly on first review), the bar may be well-calibrated or it may be too low; examine the production incident rate for the library to determine which.

# pyproject.toml — automated quality bar configuration

[tool.pytest.ini_options]
# Minimum coverage for new files
addopts = "--cov=src --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=90"
testpaths = ["tests"]

[tool.coverage.run]
# Measure branch coverage, not just line coverage
branch = true
source = ["src"]

[tool.coverage.report]
# Fail if any individual module is below 85% (overall must be 90%+)
fail_under = 90
show_missing = true

[tool.mypy]
python_version = "3.10"
strict = true  # full strict mode for shared libraries
disallow_any_generics = true
disallow_untyped_defs = true
no_implicit_optional = true

[tool.ruff]
# Style enforcement
line-length = 100
select = ["E", "W", "F", "I", "N", "D"]  # includes docstring rules (D)

# .github/workflows/quality-gates.yml
name: Quality Gates
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  # Gate 1: Tests and coverage
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ["3.10", "3.11", "3.12"]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: pytest  # coverage gate enforced by pytest config (fail_under=90)

  # Gate 2: Type checking
  typecheck:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e ".[dev]"
      - run: mypy src/

  # Gate 3: Security scan
  security:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install semgrep
      - run: semgrep --config=p/python --error  # fail on any finding

  # Gate 4: API compatibility check
  api-compat:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Check for breaking API changes
        run: |
          pip install griffe  # Python API diff tool
          griffe check agent_tool_library --against origin/main
        # Fails if public API surface changes without a MAJOR version bump

# REVIEW-STANDARDS.md — trusted committer review checklist

## Correctness
- [ ] All new public functions have tests covering: happy path, error cases, and at least
      one edge case (empty input, None, boundary values).
- [ ] Tests are meaningful — they assert specific behavior, not just that the function runs.
- [ ] Tests pass on all supported Python/Node versions in CI matrix.
- [ ] Coverage threshold is met for all new files (90% line + branch).

## API design
- [ ] New function/class names follow existing naming conventions in the library.
- [ ] New parameters are optional with sensible defaults where possible.
- [ ] The new API surface is minimal — could this be a parameter on an existing function?
- [ ] No public API from an earlier version is changed without a MAJOR version bump.
- [ ] The contribution does not duplicate functionality already in the library.

## Documentation
- [ ] All new public functions, classes, and modules have docstrings.
- [ ] Docstrings include at least one usage example.
- [ ] The CHANGELOG.md [Unreleased] section has an accurate entry.
- [ ] If the contribution adds a new dependency, it is documented in the README.

## Security
- [ ] No new subprocess calls, eval calls, or exec calls without explicit justification.
- [ ] Network calls are to approved endpoints only (check library allowlist).
- [ ] No credentials, API keys, or tokens appear in code or tests.
- [ ] SAST scan passes (enforced by CI, double-check for false negatives).

## Rejection guidance
When a checklist item is not met, reference it by name in the review comment
and provide concrete guidance: "The error case for an invalid URL is not tested.
Please add a test that verifies the function raises ToolError (not a generic Exception)
when the URL scheme is not http or https."

A contributor from Team B submits a PR adding a PDF extraction tool to the agent-tool-library. The CI pipeline runs: test coverage is 88%, below the 90% threshold, and the mypy check finds two missing type annotations on the public function's parameters. The PR is automatically marked as failing the automated gates. The contributor sees the failure details in the CI output, adds two test cases to bring coverage to 93%, and adds the missing type annotations. On resubmit, all gates pass and the PR is ready for human review.

A trusted committer reviews the PR against the human review checklist. The tests cover the happy path and the common error case (file not found) but do not test what happens when the PDF is password-protected. The API design review notes that the function's `output_format` parameter accepts a string (any string), not an enum, so callers cannot discover valid values without reading the source. The committer requests two changes: add a test for the password-protected case and change `output_format` to accept a `Literal` type with the valid options. The contributor makes both changes and the PR is approved on the second review.

The platform team uses CI metrics to track quality bar compliance across all inner-source repositories quarterly. The review shows that the agent-tool-library has an average of 1.2 review rounds per PR (close to the target of 1.5), with coverage consistently above 93% and no SAST findings in any merged PR over the quarter. The prompt-templates repository shows 2.8 review rounds per PR and several PRs that required multiple coverage fixes, indicating that contributors may not be fully aware of the coverage requirement before submitting. The platform team adds a pre-PR checklist to the CONTRIBUTING.md and links it from the PR template.

The Apache Software Foundation's meritocracy model includes explicit contribution quality requirements for projects under their governance: all code must include unit tests, documentation is required for public APIs, and committers review contributions against documented criteria before merge. The ASF's guidelines are published at community.apache.org and have been widely adopted by the open-source community. The InnerSource Commons patterns library adapts these quality bar concepts for internal organizational use, noting that the key adaptations are: publishing quality bars in the internal developer portal rather than a public community site, integrating with corporate CI/CD rather than Apache's Buildbot, and using internal metrics systems rather than public mailing list archives for program health tracking.
Setting the coverage threshold to 100%, which is expensive to achieve for complex error handling and creates contributor frustration without proportional quality benefit.
Applying the quality bar inconsistently across trusted committers; calibration sessions and the review checklist both address this.
Making the quality bar so high that small, valuable contributions (documentation fixes, additional test cases) are rejected for not meeting criteria designed for feature PRs.
Not updating the quality bar when the library grows: a bar appropriate for a 10-function library may be inadequate for a 100-function library.
Enforcing API compatibility checks without providing an automated way for contributors to check compatibility locally before submitting.
Framing rejection in terms of personal opinion rather than documented criteria, eroding contributor confidence in the fairness of the process.
Document the quality bar in the CONTRIBUTING.md and REVIEW-STANDARDS.md before the first external contribution is accepted.
Enforce as much of the quality bar as possible through automated CI gates so that human review focuses on design and context rather than mechanical checks.
Conduct periodic trusted committer calibration sessions where committers review the same sample PR independently and compare their assessments, identifying inconsistencies to resolve.
Provide a pre-PR self-assessment checklist that contributors complete before submitting, so they catch quality bar failures before the review cycle begins.
Publish quality metrics publicly within the organization: coverage trends, SAST finding rates, and average review rounds per PR create social accountability.
Distinguish between contribution types in the quality bar: documentation fixes and bug fixes should have lower barriers than new feature additions.

Automated quality gates scale without additional human effort: the same CI pipeline runs the same gates regardless of how many PRs are submitted. Human review is the non-scalable component. As contribution volume grows, the trusted committer pool must grow proportionally to maintain review SLAs. Track the ratio of open PRs to active trusted committers as a leading indicator of review capacity: if this ratio exceeds 3-4 open PRs per active committer, review latency will increase and contributor satisfaction will decrease. Add trusted committers before the ratio becomes problematic, not after.

Quality bar calibration maintenance is proportional to the library's evolution rate. A stable library with few API changes requires infrequent quality bar reviews. A rapidly evolving library requires quarterly review of whether the current bar is catching the right classes of problems. Use production incident post-mortems for shared libraries as input to quality bar evolution: if a class of bugs is reaching production that the current bar does not catch, add a criterion that addresses it.
Enforce branch coverage, not just line

Security gates in the CI pipeline are the most critical automated gates for shared libraries because a security vulnerability in a shared library has organization-wide blast radius. Configure the SAST gate to fail on findings of medium severity or above (not just high/critical), and maintain a library-specific set of custom Semgrep rules that address the most common security patterns in the library's domain — for agent tool libraries, these include URL injection in web scraping tools, path traversal in file system tools, and shell injection in subprocess-based tools. Review and update the custom rules quarterly as new contribution patterns introduce new risk surfaces.

The API compatibility gate is a reliability safeguard: it prevents breaking changes from reaching consumers without a major version bump. Configure the tool to check not just function signatures but also function semantics captured in type signatures (a function that previously accepted `None` for a parameter should not start raising an error on `None` without a major version bump). When the compatibility gate flags a change as potentially breaking, the human reviewer is responsible for making the final judgment about whether the change is truly breaking and, if so, whether the major version bump is justified.
Inner-source quality bars have two layers: automated CI gates (objective, consistent) and human review checklists (contextual, calibrated across trusted committers).
Shared library quality bars should be higher than single-team library bars because a bug or regression affects every consuming team.
Enforce as much as possible through automated gates so human review focuses on design and context, not mechanical compliance.
Document rejection reasons by referencing specific checklist criteria and providing concrete remediation guidance.
Calibrate trusted committers on the quality bar periodically through sample PR review sessions to ensure consistent application.
Review and evolve the quality bar using production incident data; the bar should catch the classes of problems that actually reach production, not just problems that are easy to automate.
